text:
Healthcare professionals bear both a legal and ethical duty to ensure the confidentiality of patient data. This obligation aligns closely with South Africa's constitutionally protected right to privacy. Patients disclose highly sensitive personal information with the expectation that it will remain private and secure. In general, revealing another individual's medical details without consent constitutes a legal and ethical violation.

However, confidentiality is not an absolute principle. The South African Constitution provides room for certain rights to be limited when it is reasonable and justifiable to do so. Under Section 36, conflicting constitutional rights—such as privacy and freedom of expression—must be balanced to serve the broader interests of justice.

Every person in South Africa holds a constitutionally recognized right to privacy, which includes protection against intrusion into private communications. This protection extends explicitly to the medical information exchanged between patients and healthcare providers. Safeguarding this information is a critical component of maintaining trust within the healthcare system.

When patients cannot trust that their information will remain confidential, they may choose to withhold essential health details. Such omissions can result in misdiagnosis, compromised care, and even harm to the patient's health outcomes.

Legal protections reinforce these ethical duties. Article 61 of the National Health Act (2003) and Article 2 of the Promotion of Access to Information Act (2000) set clear standards for access to medical records. These statutes specifically aim to secure information managed by healthcare professionals. Accountability for overseeing and protecting this data lies with designated information officers in public institutions and the heads of private healthcare entities.

Additionally, Article 4 of the Protection of Personal Information Act (2013) imposes general restrictions on the processing—collection, storage, and sharing—of personal health data. Nevertheless, the Act allows exceptions: processing is permissible if the individual provides explicit consent or if disclosure is necessary for legitimate medical, institutional, or social welfare purposes.
